strategic_profile.executive_summary: Georgetown University is an elite private Jesuit research university situated in Washington, D.C. [cite: 1]. Renowned for its proximity to national politics and global affairs, the institution is highly selective, boasting a 12.9% overall acceptance rate and prominent programs in international relations, political science, and business [cite: 2, 3].

analyst_takeaways.insight: Georgetown's Early Action round (11.1% acceptance) confers no statistical advantage over Regular Decision (12.3% acceptance) [cite: 4, 5].
analyst_takeaways.implication: Applicants should not rush an early application unless their profile is exceptionally strong by the end of junior year, as deferred EA candidates are reconsidered in the RD pool [cite: 6, 7].

analyst_takeaways.insight: The university strictly requires SAT or ACT scores and does not participate in Score Choice, demanding an applicant's complete testing record [cite: 8, 9].
analyst_takeaways.implication: Applicants cannot rely on test-optional policies to mask lower standardized test scores, underscoring Georgetown's heavy emphasis on quantitative academic metrics [cite: 8, 10].

analyst_takeaways.insight: The university lacks a Greek life system and emphasizes the Jesuit ideal of 'cura personalis' and a commitment to public service [cite: 11, 12].
analyst_takeaways.implication: Applicants must demonstrate a genuine commitment to social justice, ethics, and community involvement in their essays and alumni interviews [cite: 12].

campus_dynamics.social_environment: Vibrant, politically engaged, and heavily service-oriented community without a Greek system, fostering an inclusive environment [cite: 11, 13]. However, there is a pronounced pre-professional culture, especially for consulting and finance [cite: 13].
campus_dynamics.transportation_impact: Located steps from the nation's political center, providing exceptional access to internships on Capitol Hill, NGOs, and global institutions [cite: 13, 14].
campus_dynamics.research_impact: An R1 Doctoral University with very high research activity, prioritizing religion, ethics, science, public policy, and cancer medicine [cite: 1].

current_status.test_policy_details: SAT or ACT required. Georgetown does not participate in Score Choice and requires submission of all test scores. The university superscores both the SAT and ACT [cite: 9, 15].

longitudinal_trends.notes: Waitlist data for 2025 not yet published.
Yield rate approaches 79% when adjusting for summer melt; exceptionally high yield limits waitlist movement [cite: 4].

gpa.notes: Georgetown does not report average GPA, but 85% of enrolled students ranked in the top 10% of their high school class [cite: 16].

testing.policy_note: SAT submission rate is 78%, ACT submission rate is 30%. Required for all applicants [cite: 16].

colleges.name: Georgetown College of Arts & Sciences
colleges.strategic_fit_advice: A strong fit for students interested in humanities, natural sciences, or those who are undecided, as it offers the broadest curriculum [cite: 12].
colleges.housing_profile: 100% of first-year students live in college-owned housing [cite: 16].
colleges.student_archetype: Intellectually curious liberal arts and sciences students.

majors.name: Economics
majors.special_requirements: At least six courses must be taken in the Economics department at Georgetown [cite: 17, 18].
majors.notes: Students matriculating in Fall 2024 or after must take ECON 1357, while earlier cohorts were exempt [cite: 17, 18].

majors.name: Government
majors.special_requirements: Requires ten courses: four foundational and six electives [cite: 19, 20].
majors.notes: Students who earned a 4 or 5 on the AP exam in US Government and Politics may substitute a sequential course for GOVT 1200 [cite: 20].

colleges.name: Walsh School of Foreign Service (SFS)
colleges.strategic_fit_advice: Ideal for students highly committed to diplomacy, global policy, and international affairs [cite: 12].
colleges.housing_profile: 100% of first-year students live in college-owned housing [cite: 16].
colleges.student_archetype: Future diplomats, policymakers, and global researchers.

majors.name: International Politics
majors.special_requirements: Students must concentrate in International Law, Institutions, and Ethics; International Security; Foreign Policy and Policy Processes; or a Self-Defined area [cite: 21, 22].

electives: GOVT 4660 Ethical Issues in International Relations

majors.notes: Includes an intensive integrated writing requirement built heavily on analytical texts [cite: 23].

colleges.name: McDonough School of Business
colleges.strategic_fit_advice: For students certain about pursuing business careers, offering extremely strong placement into top consulting and finance firms [cite: 12].
colleges.housing_profile: 100% of first-year students live in college-owned housing [cite: 16].
colleges.student_archetype: Ambitious, pre-professional students aiming for Wall Street or Management Consulting.

majors.name: Finance
majors.special_requirements: Requires 15 credits in Finance above the standard business core [cite: 24].
majors.notes: Internal transfers to MSB are competitive and require accounting/business preparatory courses [cite: 25].

colleges.name: School of Health
colleges.strategic_fit_advice: Designed for students committed early to healthcare management and health sciences [cite: 12].
colleges.housing_profile: 100% of first-year students live in college-owned housing [cite: 16].
colleges.student_archetype: Future healthcare professionals and public health advocates.

colleges.name: School of Nursing
colleges.strategic_fit_advice: For students fully dedicated to nursing pathways [cite: 12].
colleges.housing_profile: 100% of first-year students live in college-owned housing [cite: 16].

application_deadlines.notes: Restrictive Early Action; applicants cannot apply binding Early Decision elsewhere [cite: 5, 6].

supplemental_requirements.details: Requires a personal/creative essay and a specific essay demonstrating fit for the distinct undergraduate school to which the student is applying [cite: 3, 12].
Optional supplemental portfolio to the respective departments [cite: 27].

holistic_factors.specific_differentiators: Georgetown places immense weight on the required alumni interview and looks closely at alignment with Jesuit values such as 'cura personalis' (care of the whole person) and community service [cite: 10, 11, 12, 27].

major_selection_tactics: Georgetown College is historically more lenient for internal transfers, making it a strategic entry point for undecided students [cite: 12, 25].
Do not apply to an 'easier' school if your extracurricular profile contradicts it; admissions readers easily spot applicants lacking genuine interest [cite: 12, 28].
If planning an internal transfer to the McDonough School of Business later, ensure you take required introductory business and accounting courses beforehand to be competitive [cite: 25].
college_ranking_tactics: The School of Foreign Service is widely perceived as the most rigorous and specialized, requiring deep, demonstrated interest in international affairs [cite: 12].
application_strategy.alternate_major_strategy: Internal transfers are allowed but require Dean's approval and a written essay. While Georgetown College is flexible, specialty schools like MSB and Nursing have strict prerequisites and GPA minimums (e.g., 3.0 for MSB) [cite: 25].

scholarships.name: 1789 Scholarships
scholarships.amount: $3,000 per year loan relief + need-based aid
scholarships.benefits: Admission to the Georgetown Scholarship Program (GSP) for top applicants.

scholarships.name: Bellarmine Scholarships
scholarships.benefits: Awarded to valedictorians from Jesuit high schools.

scholarships.name: Ignatian Scholarships
scholarships.benefits: Awarded to students in the top 5% of their Jesuit high school class who represent Ignatian ideals.

scholarships.name: Baker Trust Scholarship Program
scholarships.benefits: Supports liberal arts students in Georgetown College preparing for business careers.

ap_policy.general_rule: Awards credit and/or course exemptions for qualifying AP scores (typically 4 or 5) [cite: 17, 29].
exceptions: If a student has both AP and IB scores for the same subject, they may only apply credit from one [cite: 29].
SOH and SON students may not receive credit for more than one History course [cite: 29].
ap_policy.usage: Can be used to place out of introductory courses (e.g., Microeconomics and Macroeconomics for AP scores of 5) [cite: 17].

ib_policy.general_rule: Credit is exclusively awarded for Higher Level (HL) exams with a score of 6 or 7. No credit is awarded for Standard Level (SL) exams [cite: 29].

transfer_articulation.restrictions: Transfer students cannot bring in more than 60 total credits and must complete their remaining coursework at Georgetown [cite: 30].

what_it_takes: Exceptional academic metrics, particularly SAT/ACT scores, as Georgetown does not use a test-optional safety net [cite: 8, 10].
A demonstrated and authentic commitment to the Jesuit ideal of serving others (cura personalis) [cite: 11, 12].
A highly articulate alumni interview, which is a required and heavily weighted component [cite: 10, 27].
common_activities: Political campaigns and local government internships
Global engagement and cultural immersion programs
essay_tips: Explicitly address why the specific undergraduate school (e.g., SFS vs. Georgetown College) is the perfect fit for your academic goals [cite: 12].
Weave in personal values regarding ethics and community impact to align with Jesuit traditions [cite: 11, 12].
Do not copy and paste a Common App essay; tailor responses to the unique, standalone Georgetown prompts [cite: 12].
red_flags: Applying to the School of Foreign Service without any prior history of international or political engagement [cite: 28].
Bad-mouthing a prior institution in a transfer application [cite: 30].
Failing to articulate a clear reason for choosing a specific undergraduate college [cite: 12, 25].
insights: Georgetown's D.C. location heavily dictates its culture, with many students holding prestigious internships concurrently with classes [cite: 13, 14].
The business and foreign service programs cultivate an intense, pre-professional atmosphere that some students view as highly competitive [cite: 13].
The university's lack of Greek life fosters alternative social structures deeply tied to clubs and organizations [cite: 11, 13].
